Most Common Wastes in SMBs

A Lean-based guide to identifying non-value-added activities that are quietly costing your business time and money.

Lean Genie Advisors Inc.  |  leangenieadvisors.ca
In Lean thinking, waste (or muda) is any activity that consumes resources — time, money, or energy — without adding value for the customer. Most SMBs are losing 20–40% of their capacity to waste they can't see yet. This guide uses the proven Toyota Production System framework (TIMWOODS) to expose the most common culprits in growing businesses.

The 8 Types of Lean Waste — TIMWOODS

  • Transportation  ·  Inventory  ·  Motion  ·  Waiting
  • Overproduction  ·  Over-processing  ·  Defects  ·  Skills (underutilized talent)
Transportation

Unnecessary Movement of Materials or Information

Every time a document, product, or piece of information moves without adding value, you're wasting time and risking damage or error.

  • Routing approvals through multiple people who don't add value
  • Physically moving inventory between locations unnecessarily
  • Emailing files back and forth instead of using shared systems
💡 Typical SMB cost: 5–10% of operational time spent on non-value-added transportation of information or materials.
Inventory

Excess Stock, WIP, and Queued Work

Inventory beyond what is needed right now is waste — it ties up capital, hides problems, and occupies space and attention.

  • Overstocking raw materials or finished goods
  • Large backlogs of unprocessed requests or tickets
  • Partially completed orders sitting idle between steps
💡 Excess inventory often masks underlying process problems — it's a symptom, not just a storage issue.
Motion

Unnecessary Movement of People

Any movement that doesn't add value — walking to retrieve tools, scrolling to find files, switching between systems — is waste.

  • Workstations not ergonomically arranged
  • Frequently used items stored far from the point of use
  • Staff navigating multiple disconnected software systems for one task
💡 A study of office workers found that the average employee loses 2+ hours per day to motion waste (hunting for info, task switching).
Waiting

Idle Time Waiting for the Next Step

Waiting is one of the most visible and frustrating wastes. It happens between every hand-off in an unoptimized process.

  • Waiting for approvals, signatures, or responses
  • Staff idle while machinery or systems are down
  • Customers waiting because internal queues are backed up
💡 In many SMBs, value is only being added 10–30% of the total cycle time. The rest is waiting.
Overproduction

Producing More Than What's Needed Right Now

Overproduction is considered the most harmful waste in Lean — it creates all other wastes downstream.

  • Producing ahead of actual demand to "stay busy"
  • Preparing reports no one reads
  • Sending unnecessary communications or updates
💡 Pull-based systems (make only what is needed, when needed) reduce overproduction and free up significant capacity.
Over-Processing

Doing More Work Than the Customer Values

Adding steps, features, or polish beyond what is required by the customer is waste — even if it feels like quality improvement.

  • Triple-checking work that doesn't require it
  • Overly complex reporting or documentation
  • Using high-precision tools for tasks that don't need precision
💡 Ask: "Would the customer pay for this step?" If not, it's a candidate for elimination or simplification.
Defects

Errors, Rework, and Returns

Every defect — a wrong order, a billing error, a quality failure — requires correction, which costs 2–5x the original effort.

  • Customer complaints requiring reactive resolution
  • Internal rework loops because work wasn't done right the first time
  • Returns, refunds, or redeliveries from quality failures
💡 Most defects trace back to unclear SOPs or missing error-proofing (poka-yoke) rather than individual employee failure.
Skills

Underutilized Talent & Human Potential

This is the waste most often overlooked — when employees' knowledge, creativity, and capability go unused, the business falls behind.

  • Experienced staff doing tasks below their skill level
  • No forum for employees to suggest process improvements
  • Siloed roles that prevent cross-functional problem-solving
💡 Lean businesses create structured improvement programs (like kaizen events) where every employee contributes to operational excellence.

Let's remove non-value-added activities from your operation.
